prompt:
A developer’s laptop displays a plotting window generated by a short script: a smooth time-series line chart of temperature readings curves across the screen, with multiple days of data indicated by gentle peaks and valleys, while axis labels are abstract marks without readable text. Beside the laptop lies a small external drive or SD card labeled only with a simple log icon, and a thermistor-equipped single-board computer sits nearby, implying that the script is turning raw sensor logs into a visual graph.

The environment is a near-future solarpunk home lab: soft indirect lighting, a clean desk with pale surfaces, and a couple of plants or solar-themed decorations blurred in the background. The 512×512 photorealistic composition centers on the graph window and the modest hardware that produced the data, with notebooks and tools subtly out of focus. No brand logos or legible text appear anywhere, and no faces are shown—only a hand on a trackpad or mouse—emphasizing that a compact script is being used to plot recorded temperature readings.
